title: The 7–8% cut: when wrong, get out

diagnosticQuestions.prompt: A common O’Neil-style rule of thumb for long stock trades is:
choices: Cut losses around 7–8% below your buy point (method-dependent — verify your rules)
diagnosticQuestions.hint: Small cuts preserve capital for the next setup.

diagnosticQuestions.prompt: The point of a tight initial stop is mainly:
choices: To keep losses small enough to survive many tries

teachSlides.title: Why a line in the sand
teachSlides.body: If your thesis was a breakout and price collapses, the thesis failed. Hoping turns small losses into big ones.

teachSlides.title: Darvas and trails
teachSlides.body: Darvas trailed stops as price rose. The 7–8% idea is often about the initial buy — later you may lift the floor as price proves you right.

teachSlides.title: Your number
teachSlides.body: Some use 7%, some use ATR-based stops — pick one system and track results. The rule is discipline, not magic numbers.

recapSlide.title: Recap
recapSlide.body: Define max loss before entry. If price hits it, exit — then review cold. Small losses are tuition; huge losses are disasters.

masteryQuestions.prompt: Buying at $100 with a max 8% loss rule implies selling near:
Ignoring a stop because “it will come back” mainly risks:
choices: Turning a small loss into a career-threatening drawdown
masteryQuestions.explanation: Hope is not a risk model.

choices: Protect open profits while giving trend room
masteryQuestions.explanation: They balance giving back too much vs noise.
